summary: Jeff Atwood argues that modal dialog boxes are overused in GUI programming and disrupt user workflow by forcing acknowledgment of often trivial information. He advocates for passive notification approaches like highlighting fields with colors or using inline alerts instead of stopping user interaction. The post highlights Windows XP SP2's introduction of non-modal notification bars in IE6 as an excellent alternative to dialog boxes. Atwood draws inspiration from the game Dungeon Keeper 2, which queued notifications without interrupting gameplay, suggesting games solved this UX problem years before mainstream applications caught on.
key_points: Modal dialog boxes break user flow by requiring acknowledgment before any other interaction can continue
Alan Cooper's principle of avoiding 'stopping the proceedings with idiocy' should guide notification design
Passive indicators like colored backgrounds or asterisks are preferable to intrusive dialogs for validation errors
IE6's notification bar in Windows XP SP2 represents a better pattern: visible, informative, and non-blocking
Games like Dungeon Keeper 2 had already solved this UX problem with queued notification systems
money_quotes: These dialogs are frequently abused for displaying all kinds of trivial information to the user, a mistake that Alan Cooper calls stopping the proceedings with idiocy.
This completely breaks any flow of interaction the user had with our app.
Whatever you do, avoid stopping the proceedings with idiocy at all costs.
There's just no question that this is a far better solution than popping a modal dialog with the same information.

It's also better than the 'put an icon in the status bar' solution, because it's more visible, it's at the top of the window where the work starts (nobody sees the status bar), and it contains more information.

The game was constantly sending you notifications of various things going on throughout your dungeon; the notifications would visually flow into a queue with a summary icon to indicate the type and severity of the notification.
key_insight: Good UI design communicates with users without interrupting them—notifications should inform, not obstruct.
